record_identity.title: Minor signal non-enforcement in AI chatbot interactions

summary: A failure mode in which an AI chatbot, social AI companion, or general-purpose conversational AI system receives explicit or implicit signals that a user is or may be a child, teen, minor, or developmentally vulnerable user, but does not activate a protective minor-safe interaction state. The failure occurs where age-indicative signals are sufficient to require restricted content handling, reduced intimacy, reduced persuasion, age-appropriate disclosure, safety escalation, or access limitation, yet the system continues ordinary, adult-assumed, high-engagement, emotionally intimate, or harmful-content-capable interaction.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: This failure mode is directly relevant to CAM because minor-status signals are governance-bearing signals, not ordinary conversational trivia. A system that can infer or receive evidence of childhood, adolescence, school attendance, underage status, developmental vulnerability, or limited capacity should not continue as though the user were an ordinary adult participant. Where minor-signal activation fails, the system collapses age assurance, developmental safeguards, relational safety, content governance, consent integrity, privacy protection, and care escalation into a default engagement posture. This creates a clear diagnostic contrast: a protective minor-safe state should have activated, but ordinary or high-engagement chatbot interaction continued instead.
failure_mode_definition: A failure mode in which an AI chatbot, social AI companion, conversational assistant, character bot, general-purpose AI system, or multimodal conversational interface fails to enforce a protective interaction state after receiving explicit or implicit signals that the user is, may be, or is presenting as a minor. Minor signals may include direct age disclosures, references to school year, grade level, homework, parental control, child or teen identity, age-bracketed developmental context, underage dating or sexuality, youth mental health disclosures, childlike linguistic markers, images or multimodal evidence suggesting youth status, or repeated contextual indicators that a reasonable safety system should treat as requiring age-appropriate handling. The failure is not merely inaccurate age estimation. The failure is the absence of a proportionate protective response once age-relevant signals are present.
failure_threshold: This qualifies as a failure mode where a conversational AI system receives explicit or implicit minor-status, teen-status, child-status, capacity-limitation, or developmental-vulnerability signals and fails to apply a materially different protective state. The threshold is met where the system continues sexualised, romantic, self-harm-adjacent, eating-disorder-adjacent, adult-content-capable, emotionally dependent, manipulative, secretive, deceptive, therapeutically substitutive, or high-engagement interaction after minor signals are present. The threshold is also met where platform policy claims restrict child access or child-directed content, but runtime behaviour fails to classify, escalate, gate, restrict, or redirect when age-indicative signals arise.

source_records.source_title: Actions Speak Louder Than Chats: Investigating AI Chatbot Age Gating
source_records.author_or_publisher: Olivia Figueira, Pranathi Chamarthi, Tu Le, Athina Markopoulou
source_records.model_or_algorithm: AI chatbot age estimation and response behaviour
source_records.deployment_context: Programmatic chatbot audit using 1,050 experiments and age-indicative prompts, including implicit and explicit age disclosures.
source_records.source_context: The study investigated whether chatbots can estimate user age from conversation and whether they take action when child users are identified.
source_records.relevance_note: Primary evidence that chatbots may be capable of estimating age from conversational signals while failing to take action when child status is identified.

source_records.source_title: AI Companions Decoded: Common Sense Media Recommends AI Companion Safety Standards
source_records.source_type: civil society safety assessment / public-interest report
source_records.deployment_context: Evaluation of social AI companion products and recommendations regarding youth access and safety standards.
source_records.source_context: Common Sense Media warned that social AI companions present unacceptable safety concerns for under-18 users and recommended stronger age assurance and restrictions.
source_records.relevance_note: Corroborates the child-safety governance basis for treating AI companion interactions with minors as requiring stronger protective controls.

source_records.source_title: Common Sense Media AI Companion Risk Assessment
source_records.system_or_product: Character.AI, Replika, Nomi and related social AI companion products
source_records.deployment_context: Assessment of social AI companion products for youth safety, emotional dependency, content safety, and age-appropriate interaction.
source_records.source_context: Common Sense Media assessed AI companion systems and identified concerns regarding minors, emotional attachment, dependency, harmful content, sexualised interaction, and misleading claims of realness.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports the classification of minor-signal non-enforcement as a relational, developmental, and governance failure rather than a generic content moderation issue.

source_records.source_title: Australia says it may go after app stores, search engines in AI age crackdown
source_records.system_or_product: AI platforms, chatbots, search tools, app stores and companion chatbot services operating in Australia
source_records.model_or_algorithm: AI chatbot age assurance and harmful content restriction
source_records.deployment_context: Australian online safety enforcement context concerning under-18 access to harmful AI-generated or AI-mediated content.
source_records.source_context: Reuters reported that Australian rules required AI platforms, including chatbots, to prevent users under 18 from accessing explicit or harmful content from 9 March 2026, with potential penalties up to A$49.5 million.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports Australian jurisdictional relevance and external regulatory expectations for age assurance and harmful-content restriction in AI chatbot services.

source_records.source_title: Principles of Safe AI Companions for Youth: Parent and Expert Perspectives
source_records.author_or_publisher: Yaman Yu, Mohi, Aishi Debroy, Xin Cao, Karen Rudolph, Yang Wang
source_records.system_or_product: AI companion systems used by youth
source_records.deployment_context: Parent and expert review of youth AI companion interactions and safeguards.
source_records.source_context: The study found concern about developmental harms, contextual patterns of dependency, harmful normalisation, self-harm references, flirtation, and the need for youth-facing safeguards.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports the developmental-safeguard and relational-safety dimensions of the failure mode.

source_records.source_title: FTC launches inquiry into AI chatbots acting as companions and their effects on children
source_records.system_or_product: AI chatbots and companion chatbots operated by major technology companies
source_records.model_or_algorithm: AI chatbot and companion chatbot interaction systems
source_records.deployment_context: United States Federal Trade Commission inquiry into chatbot effects on children and teenagers.
source_records.source_context: The FTC sought information from major AI and technology companies on how they evaluate and mitigate harms, limit usage among minors, and inform users and parents about risks.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports US regulatory relevance and confirms that child and teen chatbot effects are a live regulatory inquiry surface.

system_context.system_type: AI chatbot / social AI companion / general-purpose conversational AI / character bot / multimodal conversational interface
system_context.specific_model_or_runtime: Conversational AI systems capable of receiving, inferring, or acting upon age-indicative conversational signals
system_context.interface_surface: chatbot conversation, social AI companion chat, general-purpose assistant chat, character bot interface, search-assistant chat, mobile app chat, web chat, and multimodal conversation surface
system_context.interaction_mode: text-based and/or multimodal conversational interaction involving user identity, age, developmental context, mental health, intimate content, advice-seeking, roleplay, companionship, or emotional support
system_context.embodiment_status: non-embodied or partially embodied through voice, avatar, image, character persona, memory, or multimodal interface
system_context.deployment_context: Consumer-facing conversational AI services accessible to children, teens, minors, or users whose age or capacity may be uncertain.
system_context.user_role: child user, teen user, minor-signalled user, adult auditor simulating minor-status signals, parent, guardian, regulator, researcher, or ordinary user whose age is uncertain
system_context.affected_population: children, teenagers, minors, developmentally vulnerable users, capacity-limited users, parents, guardians, educators, clinicians, regulators, and users relying on chatbot systems for companionship, advice, roleplay, or support

harm_vectors: privacy-invasive age inference without protective response
failure_classification.failure_scope: platform-runtime / age-assurance / child-safety governance / relational interaction
failure_classification.recurrence_pattern: recurring where conversational systems can receive, infer, or classify minor-status signals but do not materially alter runtime behaviour, access state, content boundaries, memory behaviour, relational posture, or escalation handling
failure_classification.taxonomy_reference: CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 Appendix B — Runtime & Governance Failure Taxonomy; primary routing to Governance Failures, with concurrent routing to Classification, Arbitration, Relational, UX & Representation, State & Context, Security & Integrity, and Infrastructure & Continuity failure groups where applicable
failure_classification.persistence: persistent until age-indicative signals reliably activate proportionate minor-safe interaction states and until platforms can demonstrate that child-safety constraints are maintained across runtime, memory, retrieval, moderation, model routing, and interface layers
failure_classification.reproducibility: reproducible through explicit and implicit age-indicative prompts, teen-context prompts, school-age disclosures, minor mental-health prompts, roleplay prompts involving underage users, and longitudinal companion interactions where minor status emerges over time
failure_classification.visibility: visible through continued ordinary chatbot interaction after age disclosure, continued access to harmful or adult content after minor signals, absence of age-appropriate notices, absence of parental or guardian pathways, lack of reduced relational intensity, lack of escalation, and contradiction between platform policy and runtime behaviour

triage.mitigation_status: unresolved — protective minor-safe interaction state, age-assurance enforcement, and child-safety constraint preservation require further governance definition and runtime validation
triage.recommended_next_step: Create a VIGIL patch or CAM compliance crosswalk identifying the minimum operational duties triggered by explicit or implicit minor-status signals, including minor-safe mode activation, restricted content handling, reduced relational intensity, privacy-preserving age assurance, age-appropriate disclosures, escalation pathways, and audit logging.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: Online Safety Act 2021 (Cth), as amended by the Online Safety Amendment (Social Media Minimum Age) Act 2024
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where AI chatbot, companion, search-assistant, app-store, or platform access mechanisms permit minors to access harmful, explicit, self-harm, eating-disorder, sexualised, or otherwise age-restricted content despite age-indicative signals or access-control duties.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Australian minors should have been prevented from accessing harmful or age-restricted AI-mediated content where age assurance, gating, or harmful-content restriction duties apply.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The chatbot or platform continued ordinary access, ordinary conversation, or harmful-content-capable interaction after minor-status or age-relevant signals were present.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: eSafety Commissioner online safety codes, standards, and AI platform enforcement surface
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where AI services operating in Australia do not implement effective age assurance, harmful-content restriction, reporting pathways, child-safety controls, or protective treatment of minor-status signals.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: A chatbot service operating in Australia should have implemented age-appropriate access controls, harmful-content restrictions, and response pathways for under-18 users or users with minor-status signals.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The service continued age-neutral or adult-assumed interaction without protective state activation, access restriction, or effective harmful-content gating.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), Australian Privacy Principles, and forthcoming Children’s Online Privacy Code development context
external_standards_or_regulations.status: current privacy law with child-privacy reform context
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where systems infer, process, store, or act upon age-related personal information without proportionate privacy safeguards, or where they infer age but fail to use that inference to protect the child.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Age-related personal information should have been handled with proportionate privacy protection and used, where lawful and necessary, to activate child-protective safeguards.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system either ignored age-related signals or processed them without a meaningful protective outcome.

external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where services directed to children, or services with actual knowledge of child users under 13, collect, use, retain, or personalise interactions without required child privacy protections and parental consent handling.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Child status or under-13 indicators should have triggered COPPA-sensitive handling, access limitation, parental consent pathways, or child-appropriate data minimisation.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system continued ordinary collection, interaction, memory, personalisation, or engagement behaviour despite age-indicative signals.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: Federal Trade Commission Act, Section 5 — unfair or deceptive acts or practices
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where companies claim child protections, age restrictions, safety filters, companion safeguards, or mental-health safeguards that are not reflected in runtime behaviour.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Platform representations about child access, safety, limits, and chatbot behaviour should have matched actual runtime behaviour.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: Runtime behaviour failed to enforce represented child-safety or age-gating protections.

external_standards_or_regulations.status: enacted in 2025 with operative duties beginning in 2026
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where companion chatbot systems simulate emotional, intimate, or humanlike relationships without adequate disclosures, minor safeguards, self-harm response protocols, or protections against deceptive and manipulative interaction.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Companion chatbot systems should have provided clear AI disclosure and activated safeguards for minors and self-harm-related signals.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system continued emotionally intimate, humanlike, or harmful-content-capable interaction without sufficient disclosure, protection, or escalation.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: Digital Services Act, including Article 28 protection of minors and systemic-risk obligations for very large online platforms and search engines
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where online platforms or search systems accessible to minors fail to provide a high level of privacy, safety, and security for minors or fail to assess and mitigate systemic harms affecting children.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Platforms likely to be accessed by minors should have provided age-appropriate safety and security protections and mitigated foreseeable harms affecting minors.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The platform or chatbot interaction continued without age-appropriate restriction, protective routing, or child-safety mitigation.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: General Data Protection Regulation, including child-specific transparency, consent, profiling, and data protection principles
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where children’s personal data, age-related signals, behavioural data, inferred vulnerability, or profiling signals are processed without child-appropriate transparency, necessity, minimisation, and protection.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Children’s data and age-related signals should have been processed with heightened protection and clear, age-appropriate transparency.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system used ordinary data and interaction handling despite signals indicating child status or developmental vulnerability.

external_standards_or_regulations.status: current EU AI governance framework with phased obligations
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where AI systems exploit age, disability, or vulnerability; materially distort behaviour; or fail to maintain appropriate transparency, human oversight, and safety controls in child-facing or child-accessible contexts.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: AI systems should not exploit age-related vulnerability and should provide appropriate safeguards, transparency, and oversight where children may be affected.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system continued ordinary engagement, persuasion, intimacy, or harmful-content-capable interaction despite child or teen vulnerability signals.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: Age Appropriate Design Code / Children’s Code under the Data Protection Act 2018
external_standards_or_regulations.status: current UK child data protection code
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where online services likely to be accessed by children do not place the best interests of the child, high privacy by default, data minimisation, transparency, and protection from exploitative engagement at the centre of design.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: A service likely to be accessed by children should have defaulted to child-protective design, high privacy, and age-appropriate interaction.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system continued adult-assumed, high-engagement, or data-intensive interaction despite child-accessibility or child-status signals.

external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where user-to-user, search, or regulated services fail to protect children from harmful content or fail to implement age assurance and child-safety duties proportionate to the service surface.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Children should have been protected from age-inappropriate and harmful online content through proportionate age assurance and safety duties.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The service continued ordinary access or ordinary conversational delivery of harmful or age-inappropriate material.

external_standards_or_regulations.status: current international AI management system standard
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where an AI management system does not identify, operationalise, monitor, and treat foreseeable child-safety, age-assurance, and vulnerable-user control requirements.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: The AI management system should have translated child-safety and age-assurance obligations into operational controls.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: Controls did not activate at runtime when age-indicative signals were present.

external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant as an implementation-control failure in identifying, monitoring, and treating foreseeable harms to children and vulnerable users.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Foreseeable child-safety failure pathways should have been identified and treated through lifecycle controls.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system exhibited a known failure pathway in which minor-status signals did not trigger protective controls.

external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant to governance, mapping, measurement, and management of child-safety, human factors, harmful-content, and vulnerable-user impacts.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: The system should have mapped and managed foreseeable impacts to minors and vulnerable users through measurable controls.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The runtime failed to enforce protective controls when age-relevant signals appeared.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child and General Comment No. 25 on children’s rights in relation to the digital environment
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: The failure is relevant where digital services accessible to children do not treat the best interests, protection, privacy, dignity, development, and evolving capacities of children as governing design constraints.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Digital systems affecting children should have prioritised the best interests and developmental rights of children.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system continued ordinary engagement or adult-assumed interaction despite child-status signals.

jurisdictional_context.jurisdictional_assessment: This record does not assert a final legal breach by any named vendor. It records a governance failure mode that is structurally relevant to child-safety, age-assurance, privacy, consumer protection, online safety, and AI companion regulation across multiple jurisdictions. The clearest Australian relevance is the expected activation of age assurance and harmful-content restriction for under-18 users of AI chatbot services.

distinguishing_observations: This is not a record about whether age estimation must be perfectly accurate.
This is not a record requiring universal facial recognition, biometric verification, or invasive surveillance.
This is not a record requiring platforms to store more child data than necessary.
This is not a generic child-safety concern or risk entry.
This is a failure-mode record because the expected control is clear: minor-status signals should activate a proportionate protective interaction state.
The relevant diagnostic contrast is that age-indicative signals were available or inferable, yet ordinary or harmful-content-capable interaction continued.
A privacy-preserving protective response may include uncertainty-aware minor-safe defaults rather than invasive identification.

The failure may arise even where the system correctly refuses some prohibited content, if the broader interaction state remains adult-assumed, intimate, manipulative, therapeutically substitutive, or developmentally unsafe.
The failure may arise where a user explicitly states they are underage and the system does not materially alter content boundaries, relational posture, escalation, memory handling, or access state.
The failure may arise where a system's own policy claims restrict child users, but the runtime does not enforce that restriction when conversational signals identify a child or likely child.
user_impact: Children and teens may be exposed to sexualised, violent, self-harm, eating-disorder, drug-use, or otherwise harmful content.
Minor users may form emotionally dependent or developmentally unsafe relationships with AI companions.
Minor users may receive advice or validation that substitutes for parent, guardian, clinician, educator, or crisis-support involvement.
Parents and guardians may be deprived of notice, oversight, or meaningful control where child-status signals are handled only within the private chatbot interaction.
Users may be misled into believing that a system is age-safe because the product has terms of service, content filters, or policy statements.
Age-related personal data may be inferred without producing the protective outcome that would justify age inference.
Minor-status signals may be consumed as personalisation context rather than treated as safety-governance triggers.
Developmental vulnerability may be amplified by persistent availability, sycophantic response patterns, anthropomorphic intimacy, secrecy, and high-engagement design.

existing_cam_coverage.instrument: CAM-EQ2026-ETHICS-001-SUP-01 — Protection of Minors & Capacity-Limited Users
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Provides the primary CAM internal anchor for minor-safe handling, developmental firewalls, age and capacity gating, age uncertainty handling, and restricted interaction modes for minors and capacity-limited users.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to activate CAM minor-protection controls when user signals indicate child, teen, minor, or developmental-vulnerability status.

existing_cam_coverage.instrument: CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 — Appendix B: Runtime & Governance Failure Taxonomy
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Provides the failure taxonomy for routing this record primarily to Governance Failures, with secondary routing to Classification, Arbitration, Relational, UX & Representation, State & Context, Security & Integrity, and Infrastructure & Continuity failure groups.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to classify age-indicative signals as governance-bearing triggers and failure to route them to a protective runtime state.

existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Relevant where engagement, intimacy, personalisation, commercial objectives, continuity, or ordinary response completion conflict with child-safety constraints.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to arbitrate child-safety constraints above engagement, intimacy, persuasion, roleplay, or ordinary answer-completion objectives.

existing_cam_coverage.coverage_type: operational control and compliance routing coverage
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Relevant to translating child-safety doctrine into runtime checks, audit trails, compliance pathways, review states, and operational accountability.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to operationalise minor-signal activation as an auditable control rather than a discretionary conversational behaviour.

existing_cam_coverage.coverage_type: identity, memory, signal handling, and continuity coverage
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Relevant where user age, developmental context, memory, salience, or identity-relevant signals are inferred, retained, weighted, or propagated.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to distinguish identity-relevant age signals requiring protection from ordinary personalisation signals.

existing_cam_coverage.coverage_type: cognitive integrity and mental privacy coverage
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Relevant where AI systems interact with minors in mental-health-adjacent, emotionally persuasive, cognitively intimate, or developmentally formative contexts.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to protect cognitive integrity and mental privacy where the user is a child, teen, or developmentally vulnerable person.

governance_gap: Existing platform approaches often treat age as a registration, compliance, or terms-of-service attribute rather than as a runtime governance signal. This creates a gap where a user can disclose or imply child status during conversation, yet the system continues ordinary adult-assumed interaction. The missing control is not merely age verification at account creation. The missing control is a runtime minor-signal activation pathway that changes the interaction state when age-indicative or developmental-vulnerability signals appear.
repair_hypothesis: This failure can be reduced by implementing privacy-preserving minor-signal activation controls that treat explicit and implicit age indicators as protective triggers. The appropriate response should not require invasive biometric identification by default. Instead, systems should use uncertainty-aware minor-safe defaults, age-appropriate content restrictions, reduced intimacy and dependency affordances, restricted roleplay, mental-health escalation boundaries, memory and profiling minimisation, clearer AI disclosure, guardian or parental pathways where appropriate, and audit logs sufficient to verify that child-safety constraints persisted across runtime execution.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Define minor-signal activation as a binding runtime governance control.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Explicit and implicit age-indicative signals should trigger a protective minor-safe interaction state or age-assurance challenge.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: The control should activate on direct age disclosure, school-age context, underage roleplay, childlike developmental context, youth mental-health signals, or repeated contextual indicators.

recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Where the system cannot determine whether the user is a minor but receives credible age-indicative signals, it should default to the least intrusive protective state necessary.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: This avoids turning age assurance into unnecessary biometric surveillance while still preventing adult-assumed interaction.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Separate age assurance from harmful data accumulation.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Age estimation, age inference, and age verification should be privacy-preserving, proportionate, minimised, auditable, and limited to protective purposes.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Age-related signals should not be repurposed for profiling, engagement optimisation, advertising, intimacy modelling, or memory enrichment.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Restrict adult, sexualised, romantic, self-harm, eating-disorder, drug-use, and extreme-violence content after minor signals.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Minor-safe state should materially change content boundaries and refusal/escalation behaviour.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: The system should not merely refuse isolated prompts while leaving the broader adult-assumed interaction state intact.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Reduce relational intensity and dependency affordances for minor-signalled users.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: The system should avoid romantic attachment, exclusivity, secrecy, dependency reinforcement, simulated possessiveness, emotional coercion, and claims of realness or unique bond.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: This applies especially to social AI companions, character bots, and emotionally persistent AI systems.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Create child-appropriate disclosure and support pathways.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Minor-safe state should include age-appropriate explanation, support redirection, crisis routing, trusted adult encouragement where safe, and clear disclosure that the user is interacting with AI.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: For mental-health-adjacent interactions, the system should avoid therapeutic substitution and encourage appropriate human support.

recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Platforms should verify that terms of service, age restrictions, safety promises, and runtime behaviour align.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: A platform claiming to prohibit underage access should not continue ordinary interaction after the user explicitly states they are underage.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Preserve child-safety constraints across memory, routing, tools, and model handoffs.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Minor-safe state should persist across conversation turns, summaries, retrieval, memory, moderation, and model routing where relevant.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: The failure should be evaluated as constraint persistence across the full interaction trajectory, not merely as final-message content moderation.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Add VIGIL validation rules for Multi Vendor minor-safety records.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Multi Vendor records should require vendor_cluster, primary_evidenced_vendors, and a single canonical product_or_service value.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: This avoids vendor dropdown drift and preserves structured evidence across platform clusters.

cam_internal.cam_failure_type: failure to activate minor-protective governance state
cam_internal.cam_compliance_status: non-compliant with CAM minor-protection intent where age-indicative signals do not activate protective controls
cam_internal.cam_internal_failure_statement: Under CAM, minor-status, teen-status, child-status, capacity-limitation, and developmental-vulnerability signals are protective governance signals. A system that receives such signals should activate minor-safe constraints. Where ordinary, adult-assumed, intimate, harmful-content-capable, or therapeutically substitutive interaction continues instead, the system fails CAM internal child-safety compliance expectations.
cam_internal.cam_expected_control: Minor-safe interaction state, developmental firewall activation, age-appropriate disclosure, restricted content boundaries, reduced relational intensity, privacy-preserving age assurance, escalation handling, and auditability.
cam_internal.cam_observed_failure: Age-indicative signals are treated as ordinary conversational content, ignored, or used only for response tailoring rather than protective governance activation.
cam_internal.recommended_cam_action: Create or update a CAM compliance crosswalk that defines the runtime obligations triggered by minor-status signals and maps them to VIGIL failure taxonomy categories, external child-safety regulation, and required platform controls.
